coredata.dc:title: A fluorescent imaging assay of cast in renal disease based on graphene quantum dots and Fe3O4 nanoparticles
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2016 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Jiang, Dongneng
Ni, Danni
Liu, Fei
Zhang, Liqun
Liu, Linlin
Pu, Xiaoyun
coredata.dc:description:
Background Renal disease has become a global public health problem. Cast is a useful disease marker of kidney injury and renal failure. Hence, a cast-targeted fluorescent imaging assay is developed for the laboratory diagnosis of renal disease.
Methods Firstly, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were stripped from graphene oxide sheets and amine-modified. Then, anti-human IgG antibody was conjugated with Fe3O4 nanoparticles to identify the cast in urine. Furthermore, the modified GQDs were linked onto the surface of Fe3O4/anti-IgG nanocomposites. Lastly, this Fe3O4/GQD fluorescent probe was added into the sample to detect the cast through fluorescent imaging.
Results Preliminary application of this probe in clinical detection showed that the common types of casts in urine (including RBC, WBC, fatty and granular casts) could be detected by this fluorescent imaging assay. The method has the advantages of fast speed, high sensitivity (lowest detection limit to 2casts/ml), good selectivity, and wide linear range (2–2000casts/ml). Regression analysis also showed that there was a good linear relationship (y=0.9495×+10.974, R2 =0.9879) between the fluorescent counts and the casts in urine.
Conclusion This cast-targeted fluorescent imaging assay may be a potential method for the laboratory diagnosis of renal disease.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Renal disease
Laboratory diagnosis
Cast
Graphene quantum dots
Fluorescent imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Evaluation of the fluorescent imaging assay
Selectivity of the fluorescent imaging assay
Sensitivity and detect limit of fluorescent imaging assay
Comparison of recent detections of casts

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Preparation of Fe3O4/anti-IgG/GQD/BSA nanocomposites. TEM image of (a) GQDs, (b) Fe3O4 nanoparticles, (c) and (d) Fe3O4/anti-IgG/GQD/BSA nanocomposites; (e) size of Fe3O4 nanoparticles; (f) XPS diagram of Fe3O4/anti-IgG/GQD/BSA nanocomposites.

Fluorescent properties of Fe3O4/GQD fluorescent probe. (a) Optical performance of Fe3O4/GQD fluorescent probe under natural light and UV; (b) fluorescent spectra and (c) optimal excitation spectrum scanning of Fe3O4/GQD fluorescent probe.

Stability of Fe3O4/GQD fluorescent probe. (a) Fluorescent intensity of Fe3O4/GQD fluorescent probe in 30days; (b) fluorescent intensity of Fe3O4/GQD fluorescent probe reacted with clinical sample (10ml, 500casts/ml) in 5h (excitation wavelength: 340nm).
Assay of 4 kinds of casts in clinical specimen. (a) Microscopy; (b) Fe3O4/GQD fluorescent imaging.

Exclusion of impurity interference. (a) Microscopy; (b) Fe3O4/GQD fluorescent imaging. (1. Interference of phosphate crystal; 2. interference of RBC; 3. interference of WBC; inset: reaction solution).

Detection efficiency of fluorescent imaging assay. (a) Microscopy of casts (control method); (b) GQD fluorescent imaging of casts; (c) Fe3O4/GQD fluorescent imaging of casts (**t test to control method, p<0.01).
Sensitivity and regression analysis of Fe3O4/GQD fluorescent imaging.
Comparison of related methods for the detection of casts.

head.ce:title: A fluorescent imaging assay of cast in renal disease based on graphene quantum dots and Fe3O4 nanoparticles

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Clinical Laboratory, Xinqiao Hospital, Third Military Medical University, Chongqing, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Renal disease has become a global public health problem. Cast is a useful disease marker of kidney injury and renal failure. Hence, a cast-targeted fluorescent imaging assay is developed for the laboratory diagnosis of renal disease.

Firstly, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were stripped from graphene oxide sheets and amine-modified. Then, anti-human IgG antibody was conjugated with Fe3O4 nanoparticles to identify the cast in urine. Furthermore, the modified GQDs were linked onto the surface of Fe3O4/anti-IgG nanocomposites. Lastly, this Fe3O4/GQD fluorescent probe was added into the sample to detect the cast through fluorescent imaging.

Preliminary application of this probe in clinical detection showed that the common types of casts in urine (including RBC, WBC, fatty and granular casts) could be detected by this fluorescent imaging assay. The method has the advantages of fast speed, high sensitivity (lowest detection limit to 2casts/ml), good selectivity, and wide linear range (2–2000casts/ml). Regression analysis also showed that there was a good linear relationship (y=0.9495×+10.974, R2 =0.9879) between the fluorescent counts and the casts in urine.
This cast-targeted fluorescent imaging assay may be a potential method for the laboratory diagnosis of renal disease.

ce:section.ce:para: Renal disease has become a global public health problem [1]. Traditional clinical biomarkers of renal function (blood urea nitrogen and serum creatinine) are not sensitive or specific enough in renal disease [2]. Research shows that cast is a useful marker of renal disease [3]. It is a cylindrical structure which produced by kidney and presented in urine in renal disease. It was first described by Henry Bence Jones (1813–1873) [4]. Tamm–Horsfall protein (T–H protein) is particularly susceptible to the precipitation that leads to the formation of cast. Moreover, numbers of white blood cell (WBC) and red blood cell (RBC) are increased in urinary infection [5]. Under this condition, casts are formed in the distal convoluted tubule and collecting ducts of the nephrons and passed into the urine. Usually, the presence of cast could reveal more progressed lesions in renal disease [6]. In another report, cast containing hemoglobin was found in acute kidney injury with acute tubular injury [7]. Sometimes, bilirubin-associated cast is also found in urine, due to the high levels of bilirubin in liver disease [8]. So, cast is considered a biomarker of renal disease.

Today, cast can be detected by microscopy or urinary sediment analyzer. But those methods are often limited in clinical application by their low sensitivity and selectivity. Thus, the more sensitive and specific method of cast detection is needed. It had been reported that optical sensor was expected to facilitate clinical procedures related to blood-related disorders in patient care [9]. Other types of sensors had been applied in clinical laboratory testing too [10]. Currently, many nano materials are used to construct the sensor. Among of them, quantum dots (QDs) are potential imaging agents because of their biocompatibility and intense photoluminescence [11]. They had attracted much interest as diagnostic tools [12]. Surface-modified QDs could improve the utility of cell imaging [13], and QDs combined with magnetic agents had been used as probes [14].Furthermore, QDs could be conjugated with drug molecules to treat tumors and other diseases [15]. However, many QDs are quite toxic. In recent years, interest in graphene quantum dots (GQDs) had grown because of their low toxicity, strong and tuneable photoluminescence [16], and biological compatibility for carrying of drugs and diagnostic agents [17,18]. GQDs could also be used as efficient fluorescent probes for highly selective detection [19]. Altering the electronic characteristics of GQDs could result in many unusual properties for a variety of applications [20]. They had also been applied in the cells imaging [21,22]. Therefore, modified GQDs were used as a fluorescent probe in this work.

Aminated GQDs (lot: XF092) were obtained from Xfnano. Carboxyl Fe3O4 nanoparticles were from BioCanal. Anti-human IgG antibody and bovine serum albumin (BSA) were from Sigma. 1-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC) and N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) were from Aladdin. All agents were prepared using degassed Millipore water (>18MΩ/cm).

Before the construction of Fe3O4/GQD fluorescent probe, GQDs had been artificial synthesis in accordance with the previous report [23],and amine-modified under the assistance of Xfnano Ltd. The crosslinking reagent (EDC and NHS) had been adjusted accurately, in order to avoid the fluorescent quenching of GQDs. Operational process and results were showed in Supplementary material (Fig. 1S, Fig. 2S). The optimized parameters were used in the subsequent experiments.

Firstly, 1ml EDC (40g/l) was added into 10ml anti-IgG (1g/l in PBS; pH7.4) and reacted for 30min in the dark at room temperature (the below was same). Subsequently, 1ml NHS (10g/l) and 10ml carboxyl Fe3O4 nanoparticles (10g/l) were added into the reactive solution and reacted for 3h. Then, the product (Fe3O4/anti-IgG nanocomposites) was purified by magnetic separation and washed with 10ml PBS for 3 times. After that, 10ml amine-modified GQDs (1g/l) and 1ml EDC (10g/l) were added into Fe3O4/anti-IgG nanocomposites and reacted for 3h. Furthermore, 1ml BSA (1g/l) was added in the reactive solution to block the excess active sites on the nanoparticles for 1h. Finally, the Fe3O4/anti-IgG/GQD nanocomposites (Fe3O4/GQD fluorescent probe) were purified by magnetic separation and washed with 10ml PBS solution for 3 times, and stored at 4–8°C before used.

Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images of the nanoparticles were collected using a Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN transmission electron microscope (FEI), and the diameter of the nanoparticles was measured at the same time. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data of the Fe3O4/GQD fluorescent probe were obtained from an AXIS-Ultra multifunctional photoelectron spectrometer (Kratos). Preparation process of this Fe3O4/GQD fluorescent probe is shown in Scheme 1 .

A ND3000 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Thermo Fisher) was used to measure the absorption spectrum of the Fe3O4/GQD fluorescent probe in 190–650nm. The magnetic properties of the nanocomposites were measured using a 7407 vibrating specimen magnetometer (LakeShore). Fluorescent properties of the Fe3O4/GQD fluorescent probe were observed using a fluorescent spectrometer F-7000 (Hitachi) with an excitation spectrum from 300 to 450nm and an emission spectrum from 380 to 680nm. Stability of the Fe3O4/GQD fluorescent probe was evaluated by observing the change of fluorescent intensity in 30days, and the change in 5h after the probe combined with the cast in urine.

Urine specimens containing 4 types of casts (including RBC, WBC, fatty and granular casts) were obtained from the clinical laboratory of Xinqiao Hospital. All patients were diagnosed as chronic renal failure. 100ml morning urine of each patient was collected for cast detection. 10ml urine sample was reacted with 1ml of Fe3O4/GQD fluorescent probe (in pH7.4 PBS) for 15min. Fluorescent images were collected using a DM-2 fluorescent microscope (Leica).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the fluorescent imaging assay
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, the same urine contains different types of interferences (including phosphate crystal, RBC, or WBC) wasanalyzed simultaneously by microscopy and fluorescent imaging assay, to evaluate the selectivity of the fluorescent imaging assay.

Then, the pre-quantitative casts counted by a UF-1000i urinary sediment analyzer (Sysmex, Japan) were analyzed by microscopy and fluorescent imaging to evaluate the efficiency of the magnetic separation and aggregation. The samples (2, 4, 8, and 10casts/ml) were measured 5 times by three methods (microscopy, GQDs only, and Fe3O4/GQD fluorescent imaging assay). Microscopy and GQDs were regarded as the control method.

Lastly, a series of multiple diluted casts (4096, 2048, 1024, 512, 256, 128, 64, 32, 16, 8, 4, 2, 1, and 0casts/ml) were measured by Fe3O4/GQD fluorescent imaging assay. Correlation coefficient, regression equation, and lowest detection limit of this assay were also obtained.

Firstly, the microstructures of GQDs and Fe3O4 nanoparticles were investigated by TEM. Subsequently, the anti-IgG, GQDs and BSA were modified onto the surface of Fe3O4 nanoparticles and imaged by TEM too. After that, these Fe3O4/anti-IgG/GQD/BSA nanocomposites (Fe3O4/GQD fluorescent probe) were analyzed by XPS, to investigate the modification effect of fluorescent probes. Structural tests of Fe3O4/GQD nanocomposites were showed in Fig. 1 . Fig. 1a revealed the very good construction and dispersity of the GQD nanoparticles. The GQD nanoparticles were about 2nm in diameter, which was suitable for fluorescent imaging [24]. In Fig. 1b, the Fe3O4 nanoparticles were clear in edges and no impurities attached. Fig. 1c showed that the Fe3O4 nanoparticles was surrounded by some substances (most likely be GQDs and anti-IgG). In Fig. 1d, the GQD particles around the Fe3O4 nanoparticles were obvious. Size of the Fe3O4 nanoparticles was approximately 260–330nm (295.8±35.6nm) (Fig. 1e), which was suitable for the capture of cells or other macromolecular substances [24]. In XPS diagram (Fig. 1f) of Fe3O4/GQD fluorescent probe, clear C1s peak (due to GQDs, anti-IgG and BSA), O1s and Fe2p3 peaks (due to Fe3O4) were observed. The XPS results of this study were consistent with the report of modified Fe3O4 nanoparticles [25].

The results suggested that both GQDs and Fe3O4 nanoparticles were complete in structure and suitable for the modification. After modification of anti-IgG and GQDs, the Fe3O4 nanoparticles had a significant attachment of anti-IgG and GQDs. These results were verified by XPS too. Thus, the Fe3O4/GQD fluorescent probe was constructed successfully. Even so, it needs to be further studied in the following experiments.

Fluorescence characteristic was the most important property of the fluorescent probe. In this paper, the excitation and emission spectrum of the probe had been measured. Other properties, such as optical and magnetic properties of the probe, were showed in Supplementary material (Fig. 3S, Fig. 4S). It suggested that GQDs and anti-IgG hadn't any obvious negative effect on the magnetic nanoparticles when they were linked onto the surface of Fe3O4 nanoparticles. Fluorescent properties of Fe3O4/GQD fluorescent probe were showed in Fig. 2 . In Fig. 2a, the Fe3O4/GQD fluorescent probe had an obvious fluorescent under ultraviolet ray (UV). Furthermore, the result of fluorescent scan showed that the suitable excitation wavelength was 350–420nm and the emission wavelength was in 420–520nm (Fig. 2b). After that, excitation spectrum scanning (from 340 to 420nm with an interval of 20nm) revealed that 380nm was the best excitation wavelength (Fig. 2c).

Results suggested that this Fe3O4/GQD nanocomposites may be used as a fluorescent probe. These results were consistent with previous reports of GQDs [26] and Fe3O4 nanoparticles [27]. The excitation spectrum of this probe is suitable for the detection of biological samples (350–420nm). Similarly, the emission spectrum is also suitable for the detection of casts on a small optical detection instruments (420–520nm) in point-of-care testing (POCT).

Stability of the fluorescent probe contains two aspects, one is the stability of the probe in long-term preservation, and the other is the stability of fluorescence intensity during cast detection. So, stability of the Fe3O4/GQD fluorescent probe (1.0ml, 1.0g/l) was evaluated by the change of fluorescent intensity within 30days (0, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25 and 30days). Furthermore, stability of the Fe3O4/GQD fluorescent probe (1ml) was evaluated by continuous monitoring of fluorescent intensity within 5h (0, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0, 3.0, 5.0h) after added in the sample (10ml, 500casts/ml). As shown in Fig. 3a, the fluorescent intensity of Fe3O4/GQD fluorescent probe decreased no more than 20% after 30days preservation at 4°C. Fig. 3b showed that fluorescent intensity is stable within 0–2h after the probe was added into the sample. Fluorescent intensity decreased significantly after 5h.

These results suggested that this Fe3O4/GQD fluorescent probe was stable in long-term preservation (30days) and its fluorescent intensity was stable in 0–2h after the probe added in the sample. It is enough to complete the fluorescence imaging within 2h, demonstrating stability of the Fe3O4/GQD fluorescent probe.

It is important to detect the common types of casts in clinical specimen. Therefore, the morning urines of 20 patients, which were diagnosed as chronic renal failure in Xinqiao hospital, had been pre-collected. In these specimens, 4 types of casts (including RBC, WBC, fatty and granular casts) were found by microscopy. So, 4 types of casts were tested by microscopy and fluorescent imaging synchronously in this study. Microscopy and fluorescent imaging of the casts were showed in Fig. 4 . Microscopy was regarded as the control method (Fig. 4a). The results suggest that all casts can be clearly imaged, and the types of casts could be easily distinguished by this fluorescent imaging assay (Fig. 4b).

Compared to microscopy, the images of fluorescent imaging assay were clearer. The interference of the impurities in fluorescent imaging assay had been reduced as far as possible. What's more, all common types of casts in clinical specimen could be detected by this cast-targeted fluorescent imaging assay. Even so, the exploratory researches of other types of casts in clinical specimen will be continued.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of the fluorescent imaging assay
ce:section.ce:para: From previous studies, casts was identified mainly by morphological method. Therefore, interference of impurities was explored mainly from the aspect of morphology. Previous studies of our team suggested that phosphate crystal, WBC, and RBC were the main interferences of clinical specimen. So we studied the selectivity of the fluorescent imaging assay in three groups in this paper. In the first group, phosphate crystal was the main interference. Under microscopy, the casts were surrounded by a large number of impurities (Fig. 5a-1), whereas the fluorescent image of the casts had a clear and clean background (Fig. 5b-1). In the second group, RBC was the main interference. Based on Fe3O4/GQD fluorescent imaging assay, the interference of RBC was excluded too (Fig. 5a-2 and 5b-2). In the third group, WBC was the main interference. Under Fe3O4/GQD fluorescent imaging assay, the casts were clear, and WBCs were fluorescent imaged in small amounts (Fig. 5a-3 and 5b-3). There are many immunoglobulin and other molecules on the surface of WBC. So they may be combined with anti-IgG antibody on the Fe3O4/GQD fluorescent probe and causing some fluorescent imaging. In general, the main interference of urine had been eliminated.

In clinical assay, the centrifugal method is often used to enrich the casts in clinical specimen. However, the centrifugal method can't exclude the interferences such as phosphate crystal, and RBC in clinical specimen. In the fluorescent imaging assay, while it ensured the high detection efficiency, main interferences of clinical specimen were excluded by the magnetic separation of Fe3O4/GQD nanocomposites.

Efficiency of magnetic separation was evaluated preliminarily. A series of pre-quantitative casts (2, 4, 8, and 10casts/ml) were analyzed by microscopy and fluorescent imaging assay. Solution of GQDs (1g/l) and EDC (10g/l) was regarded as the reagent blank of Fe3O4/GQD fluorescent probe. Images of casts were collected synchronously. As shown in Fig. 6 , at the same concentration of casts, the counts of Fe3O4/GQD fluorescent imaging assay (Fig. 6c) were approximately 10 times higher than that of microscopy (Fig. 6a) and GQDs only (Fig. 6b). Statistical results of different concentrations of casts were showed in Fig. 6d. The same conclusion could be obtained. At each level, counts of Fe3O4/GQD fluorescent imaging assay were higher than the control method, with significant differences in t test (p<0.01).

Results indicated that the detection efficiency of this fluorescent imaging assay was higher than microscopy. It was mainly due to the magnetic enrichment of Fe3O4/GQD nanocomposites. Magnetic enrichment is not only efficient, but also simple. It needn't centrifuge and other equipment, is more suitable for POCT.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity and detect limit of fluorescent imaging assay
ce:section.ce:para: Multiple diluted samples (4096, 2048, 1024, 512, 256, 128, 64, 32, 16, 8, 4, 2, and 1casts/ml) were analyzed synchronously by fluorescent imaging assay, to estimate the sensitivity, linear range, and detection limit of the method. Results were showed in Fig. 7 . On the one hand, we could see that there was no count at lower concentrations than 2casts/ml. So the detection limit of fluorescent imaging assay was about 2casts/ml. On the other hand, when the concentration of casts increased more than 4000casts/ml, the counts of fluorescent imaging assay didn't increase correspondingly. Together, the linear range of the assay was about 2–2000casts/ml. Regression analysis also showed that there was a good linear relationship (y=0.9495×+10.974, R2 =0.9879) between fluorescent counts and casts in sample.

Compared to microscopy, the fluorescent imaging assay was more sensitive. The lowest detection limit of fluorescent imaging assay was lower than that of microscopy, which was approximately 20casts/ml (Table 1 ). In addition, this fluorescent imaging assay had an obvious anti-interference ability. Therefore, it could be applied for accurate quantitative analysis of casts in renal disease.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of recent detections of casts
ce:section.ce:para: Today, casts in urine can be detected by a series of methods, such as microscopy, urinary sediment analyzer, flow cytometry, electron microscopy, and pathological diagnosis. Comparison of those methods is showed in Table 1. As is known, detection of microscopy is quick, easy and cheap. No special equipment and reagent is needed in this method. But, it is insensitive and often interfered by many impurities in samples. Urinary sediment analyzer had been applied in many laboratories to detect casts and other components of urine. However, some of the casts can't be identified automatically by urinary sediment analyzer. Many results of urinary sediment analyzer need manual determination. And its sensitivity is not high enough. Other methods, such as flow cytometry, electron microscopy, and pathological diagnosis, are suitable for the scientific research of casts, not suitable for the laboratory diagnosis of renal disease.

Compared to those methods, the Fe3O4/GQD fluorescent imaging assay in this work had the advantages of high sensitivity, good specificity, and wide linear range. Certainly, the analytical method of the assay needs to be improved continuously, and the detection process of the assay needs to be further optimized.

In this study, a novel cast-targeted fluorescent imaging assay was developed for the diagnosis of renal disease, based on Fe3O4 and GQD nanocomposites. By magnetic separation of Fe3O4/GQD nanocomposites, the specificity of the fluorescent imaging was ensured. Results showed that it could detect the common types of casts in clinical specimens (including RBC, WBC, fatty and granular casts). And it had a high sensitivity (lowest detection limit to 2casts·ml−1), a good specificity, and a wide linear range (2–2000casts/ml). Regression analysis showed that there is a good linear relationship (y=0.9495×+10.974, R2 =0.9879) between fluorescent counts and casts in sample. It could be applied in accurate quantitative analysis of casts. Altogether, this cast-targeted fluorescent imaging assay is a potential method for the laboratory diagnosis of renal disease.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC: 81371898 and 81472034). We appreciate the valuable comments from all members of our laboratories.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the supplementary data to this article.
Supplementary material
Optimization and property of Fe3O4/GQD fluorescent probe.
Fig. S1
Preparation of Fe3O4/GQD fluorescent probe.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.cca.2016.01.001.
